items.subtopic: B. Advanced Cancers with Insufficient Data to Support Host-Derived T Lymphocyte or Immunoregulatory Antibody Treatment $ ^{d} $ (part 2)
items.title: Talimogene Laherparepvec: Oncolytic Virus for Melanoma
items.content: Talimogene laherparepvec is an attenuated herpes virus approved for stimulating immune responses in melanoma. It is instilled locally into melanoma deposits, with minimal systemic effects, and can be considered especially when other immunoregulatory antibodies are ineffective.
keyPoints: Approved for use in melanoma
Acts as an oncolytic virus to stimulate local immune response
Minimal systemic effects due to local instillation
Considered for tumors not responsive to approved immunoregulatory antibodies
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · B. Advanced Cancers with Insufficient Data to Support Host-Derived T Lymphocyte or Immunoregulatory Antibody Treatment $ ^{d} $ (part 2)

items.title: Mechanism and Toxicity of Alkylating Agents
items.content: Alkylating agents are phase-nonspecific chemotherapeutic drugs that modify DNA bases, leading to strand cross-linkage or breaks. This damage triggers apoptosis by preventing normal cell division. Common toxicities include myelosuppression, alopecia, gonadal dysfunction, mucositis, and pulmonary fibrosis. Long-term use can lead to secondary neoplasms like leukemia.
keyPoints: Alkylating agents are phase-nonspecific
Toxicities: myelosuppression, alopecia, gonadal dysfunction, mucositis, pulmonary fibrosis
Potential for causing 'second' neoplasms, particularly leukemia
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER CYTOTOXIC THERAPY (part 1)

items.title: Clinical Considerations for Cyclophosphamide Use
items.content: Cyclophosphamide requires hepatic metabolism to become active, producing both therapeutic and toxic metabolites. Adequate hydration is essential to prevent chemical cystitis, which can be mitigated by mesna. Liver disease affects drug activation, and high doses may lead to pulmonary fibrosis or cardiac dysfunction.
keyPoints: Cyclophosphamide requires liver metabolism
Hydration prevents chemical cystitis; mesna can mitigate it
Liver disease impairs activation; high doses risk pulmonary fibrosis or cardiac dysfunction
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER CYTOTOXIC THERAPY (part 1)

items.title: Selective Susceptibility of Brain Tumors to Temozolomide
items.content: Temozolomide, activated by non-enzymatic hydrolysis in tumors, is orally bioavailable and selectively targets brain tumors with alkylguanine alkyl transferase deficiency. It alkylates the O6 position of guanine, making it effective against certain brain malignancies.
keyPoints: Temozolomide activated by non-enzymatic hydrolysis
Orally bioavailable
Selective for brain tumors with alkylguanine alkyl transferase deficiency
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER CYTOTOXIC THERAPY (part 1)

items.title: Mechanism and Toxicity of Cisplatin
items.content: Cisplatin is a platinum-based antitumor agent that forms Pt-DNA cross-links by losing chloride ions in tumor cells, leading to DNA interaction. It requires hydration with forced diuresis using mannitol to prevent kidney damage, though gradual renal function decline and anemia are common side effects.
keyPoints: Cisplatin's active form is cis diamine configuration.
Preventive measures include abundant hydration and forced diuresis with mannitol.
Common toxicities: nephrotoxicity (gradual kidney damage), anemia, hypomagnesemia leading to hypocalcemia and tetany, neurotoxicity, and hearing loss in 50% of patients at conventional doses.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER CYTOTOXIC THERAPY (part 2)

items.title: Doxorubicin's Cardiotoxicity Management
items.content: Doxorubicin causes predictable myelosuppression, alopecia, nausea, and mucositis. Its cardiotoxicity is dose-dependent, with chronic cardiomyopathy risk increasing at cumulative doses >550 mg/m². Dexrazoxane can mitigate this risk, especially when combined with trastuzumab.
keyPoints: Cumulative doxorubicin dose >550 mg/m² leads to a 10% incidence of chronic cardiomyopathy.
Cardiotoxicity is related to peak serum concentration; low-dose frequent treatment or continuous infusions are better tolerated.
Dexrazoxane acts as a cardio-protectant, especially when doxorubicin is used with trastuzumab.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER CYTOTOXIC THERAPY (part 2)

items.title: Mitoxantrone's Cardiotoxicity Profile
items.content: Mitoxantrone, a synthetic topoisomerase II-directed agent similar to anthracyclines, has less cardiotoxicity but still poses a risk with a 10% incidence at cumulative doses >150 mg/m².
keyPoints: Mitoxantrone's cardiotoxicity is lower compared to anthracyclines but significant at cumulative doses >150 mg/m².
The ratio of cardiotoxic to effective doses is less than that of anthracyclines.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER CYTOTOXIC THERAPY (part 2)

items.title: Irinotecan Metabolism and Toxicity in Gilbert's Disease
items.content: Irinotecan is metabolized to its active form, SN-38, primarily in the liver. In patients with Gilbert's disease, characterized by defective UGT1A1 enzyme activity leading to indirect hyperbilirubinemia, levels of SN-38 are significantly elevated. This condition affects approximately 10% of the white population in the United States and influences irinotecan's myelosuppressive effects.
keyPoints: Irinotecan is a prodrug metabolized to SN-38.
SN-38 levels are high in Gilbert's disease due to defective UGT1A1.
~10% of the white population in the US has Gilbert's disease.
Myelosuppression by irinotecan is influenced by UGT1As genotype.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER CYTOTOXIC THERAPY (part 3)

items.title: Bleomycin-Induced Pulmonary Fibrosis
items.content: Bleomycin, used in curative regimens for Hodgkin's lymphoma and germ cell neoplasms, can cause pulmonary fibrosis, a serious complication. The risk increases with cumulative doses exceeding 300 units. Early indicators of toxicity include decreased DLCO or coughing. Pulmonary function may continue to decline even after stopping the drug upon detecting reduced DLCO.
keyPoints: Bleomycin causes pulmonary fibrosis at >300 cumulative units.
Pulmonary fibrosis is minimally responsive to treatment (e.g., glucocorticoids).
Early indicators: decreased DLCO or coughing.
Stop drug on reduced DLCO may not prevent further decline.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER CYTOTOXIC THERAPY (part 3)

items.title: Methotrexate Clearance and Toxicity Management
items.content: Methotrexate is cleared renally, with toxicity exacerbated by renal dysfunction. High-dose regimens require urine alkalinization to prevent crystallization in renal tubules. Leucovorin rescue is used to mitigate methotrexate's effects on normal cells, with specific dosing adjustments based on creatinine clearance.
keyPoints: Methotrexate cleared by kidney via glomerular filtration and tubular secretion.
Toxicity increased by renal dysfunction and certain drugs (e.g., salicylates).
15 mg/m² leucovorin rescues 10^{-8}-10^{-6} M methotrexate with normal renal function.
50–100 mg/m² leucovorin for decreased creatinine clearance until <5 × 10^{-8} M methotrexate.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER CYTOTOXIC THERAPY (part 3)

items.title: Mechanism of Action and Toxicity Profile of 5-Fluorouracil
items.content: 5-Fluorouracil (5-FU) is metabolized into 5'FdUMP, inhibiting thymidylate synthase (TS), leading to DNA misincorporation and single-strand breaks. Deficiency in dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase can cause excessive toxicity. Leucovorin enhances its activity by promoting the ternary complex formation with TS.
keyPoints: 5-FU inhibits thymidylate synthase (TS) via 5'FdUMP.
Deficiency in dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase increases toxicity risk.
Leucovorin augments 5-FU activity by promoting ternary complex formation with TS.
Toxicities include bone marrow suppression, stomatitis, CNS dysfunction, and endothelial toxicity.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER CYTOTOXIC THERAPY (part 4)

items.title: Gemcitabine's Unique Mechanism and Clinical Utility
items.content: Gemcitabine is incorporated into DNA as a triphosphate, causing inefficiently repaired DNA breakage. Unlike cytosine arabinoside, it shows efficacy in various solid tumors with limited nonmyelosuppressive toxicities.
keyPoints: Gemcitabine causes DNA damage through incorporation as a triphosphate.
DNA lesions from gemcitabine are inefficiently repaired.
Effective against multiple solid tumors with fewer nonmyelosuppressive side effects.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER CYTOTOXIC THERAPY (part 4)

items.title: 6-Mercaptopurine Metabolism and Drug Interactions
items.content: 6-Mercaptopurine is metabolized by xanthine oxidase, requiring dose adjustment with allopurinol. Thiopurine methyltransferase deficiency leads to excessive toxicity.
keyPoints: 6-MP metabolism involves xanthine oxidase and thiopurine methyltransferase.
Dose reduction needed when co-administered with allopurinol due to interaction with xanthine oxidase.
Thiopurine methyltransferase deficiency results in increased toxicity.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER CYTOTOXIC THERAPY (part 4)

items.title: Mechanism and Use of Taxanes in Cancer Therapy
items.content: Taxanes, including paclitaxel and docetaxel, stabilize microtubules against depolymerization, disrupting cell cycle completion. They are broadly active against solid tumors such as ovarian, breast, prostate, non-small cell lung cancers, and Kaposi's sarcoma.
keyPoints: Taxanes stabilize microtubules against depolymerization.
Used for ovarian, breast, prostate, non-small cell lung cancers, and Kaposi's sarcoma.
Administered intravenously; risk of hypersensitivity reactions mitigated by premedication with dexamethasone (8–16 mg), diphenhydramine (50 mg), and cimetidine (300 mg).
Paclitaxel side effects include myelosuppression, neurotoxicity (glove-and-stocking numbness, paresthesia).
Docetaxel causes similar side effects plus fluid retention.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER CYTOTOXIC THERAPY (part 5)

items.title: Epothilones and Eribulin in Taxane-Resistant Cancers
items.content: Epothilones, such as ixabepilone, are effective against taxane-resistant tumors, particularly breast cancers. Eribulin is used for patients with disease progression on taxanes, altering microtubule remodeling.
keyPoints: Ixabepilone active in taxane and anthracycline-resistant breast cancers.
Eribulin used after progression on taxanes; alters microtubule dynamics.
Both agents cause myelosuppression and peripheral sensory neuropathy.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER CYTOTOXIC THERAPY (part 5)

items.title: Mechanism of Action and Safety Concerns in ADCs
items.content: Antibody-drug conjugates (ADCs) enhance antitumor effects by linking cytotoxic agents to humanized monoclonal antibodies. The release mechanism, determined by the linker's nature, can be either intracellular or extracellular, allowing for 'bystander' cell killing. Selecting antibodies with high tumor cell selectivity is crucial to minimize damage to normal tissues.
keyPoints: ADCs link cytotoxic agents to monoclonal antibodies via linkers.
Linker determines if the cytotoxic agent is released intracellularly or extracellularly.
High tumor cell selectivity of antibodies is essential to avoid damage to normal tissue.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · ANTIBODY-DRUG CONJUGATES

items.title: Clinical Use and Monitoring of ADCs
items.content: ADCs are categorized based on their cytotoxic payload, targeting DNA structure, microtubules, or topoisomerase I. Each class has specific clinical applications and potential side effects that require vigilant monitoring. For example, calicheamicin-based ADCs can cause hepatotoxicity, while maytansine derivatives necessitate cardiac monitoring.
keyPoints: ADCs targeting DNA include gemtuzumab ozogamicin for CD33+ AML and inotuzumab ozogamicin for CD22+ ALL.
Maytansine derivatives like ado-trastuzumab emtansine are used for trastuzumab-resistant breast cancer.
Topoisomerase I-directed ADCs such as fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki target HER2-positive neoplasms.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · ANTIBODY-DRUG CONJUGATES

items.title: Role of Molecular Testing in Targeted Therapy
items.content: Molecular targeted therapies require precise identification of specific mutations or molecular targets present in cancer cells. This necessitates refined diagnostic strategies, including initial and possibly repeated biopsies, as well as the use of liquid biopsies to monitor circulating tumor DNA.
keyPoints: Therapies are directed at specific cancer cell molecular targets important for tumor proliferation.
Use must be guided by refined diagnostic strategies involving initial and repeated biopsies or liquid biopsies.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER MOLECULAR TARGETED THERAPY (part 1)

items.title: Hormone Receptor-Directed Therapy in Cancer
items.content: Steroid hormone receptor-related molecules are among the first classes of molecular target anticancer drugs. They alter gene transcription when bound to ligands, affecting tumor cell growth and death. Specific therapies include glucocorticoids for leukemias and lymphomas, tamoxifen for ER-positive breast cancer, and aromatase inhibitors for postmenopausal breast cancer.
keyPoints: Glucocorticoids induce cell death in leukemias and lymphomas but can cause Cushing’s syndrome and infections like Pneumocystis pneumonia.
Tamoxifen is a partial ER antagonist, increasing risks of thromboembolic phenomena and endometrial carcinoma after >5 years of use.
Anastrozole is superior to tamoxifen in postmenopausal patients with ER-positive breast cancer.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER MOLECULAR TARGETED THERAPY (part 1)

items.title: Androgen Deprivation Therapy for Prostate Cancer
items.content: Metastatic prostate cancer is primarily treated by androgen deprivation. Options include orchiectomy, which causes responses in 80% of patients, or LHRH agonists like leuprolide and goserelin to suppress testicular androgens. Enzalutamide binds to the androgen receptor, antagonizing its action distinctively.
keyPoints: Orchiectomy results in responses in 80% of metastatic prostate cancer patients.
LHRH agonists like leuprolide and goserelin decrease LH output by the anterior pituitary.
Enzalutamide antagonizes androgen action at the receptor level.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER MOLECULAR TARGETED THERAPY (part 1)

items.title: Hormonal Manipulation Strategies in Cancer Treatment
items.content: Certain tumors respond to sequential hormonal manipulations. For instance, breast tumors previously treated with tamoxifen may benefit from withdrawal of tamoxifen or addition of an aromatase inhibitor or progestin upon relapse. Similarly, prostate cancers initially managed with leuprolide plus flutamide might show response upon flutamide withdrawal after disease progression.
keyPoints: Sequential hormonal manipulations can be effective in certain tumors.
Breast tumors may respond to tamoxifen withdrawal or addition of aromatase inhibitors/progestin after relapse.
Prostate cancers treated with leuprolide plus flutamide might benefit from flutamide withdrawal post-progression.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER MOLECULAR TARGETED THERAPY (part 2)

items.title: Imatinib in Hematologic Malignancies
items.content: Imatinib targets the ATP binding site of the p210^bcr-abl protein tyrosine kinase, crucial in chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) due to the Philadelphia chromosome. It is also effective in gastrointestinal stromal tumors (GIST) and certain myeloproliferative disorders. However, its efficacy diminishes in CML's blast phase due to additional mutations.
keyPoints: Imatinib targets p210^bcr-abl protein tyrosine kinase in CML.
Effective for GIST and some myeloproliferative disorders.
Reduced activity in CML blast phase due to additional mutations.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER MOLECULAR TARGETED THERAPY (part 2)

items.title: BTK Inhibitors in Lymphomas
items.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, such as ibrutinib and acalabrutinib, are effective against certain lymphomas. These agents require prophylaxis for tumor lysis syndrome due to their robust lympholytic effects. Resistance often involves a C481S mutation, where noncovalent inhibitors like pirtobrutinib may be beneficial.
keyPoints: BTK inhibitors (ibrutinib, acalabrutinib) are active in certain lymphomas.
Prophylaxis for tumor lysis syndrome is necessary due to robust effects.
Resistance often involves C481S mutation; pirtobrutinib may be effective.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER MOLECULAR TARGETED THERAPY (part 2)

items.title: EGFR Mutation Profiling in NSCLC
items.content: Profiling for sensitizing EGFR mutations is crucial in NSCLC to predict response to gefitinib or erlotinib. Patients with these mutations show markedly increased response rates.
keyPoints: Sensitizing EGFR mutations increase the probability of response to gefitinib and erlotinib.
Side effects include acneiform rash (managed with glucocorticoid creams and clindamycin gel) and diarrhea.
Resistance can develop through additional mutations in EGFR, similar to imatinib resistance in CML.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER MOLECULAR TARGETED THERAPY (part 3)

items.title: ALK and RET Oncogene Targeting
items.content: Targeted therapies for ALK and RET mutations in NSCLCs include alectinib, ceritinib, crizotinib, lorlatinib, selpercatinib, and repotrectinib. These agents have specific adverse effects.
keyPoints: ALK inhibitors (alectinib, ceritinib, crizotinib, lorlatinib) target ALK mutations in NSCLC.
Lorlatinib can cause pulmonary events; alectinib and others may lead to cardiac and metabolic issues.
Selpercatinib targets RET mutations in NSCLCs and thyroid cancers with potential cardiac and liver toxicity.
Repotrectinib is effective against ROS1 mutant NSCLC.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER MOLECULAR TARGETED THERAPY (part 3)

items.title: Histology Agnostic Agents for NRTK Gene Alterations
items.content: Larotrectinib and entrectinib are effective against tumors with specific NRTK gene alterations, functioning as histology agnostic agents.
keyPoints: NRTK translocations produce chimeric proteins in various solid tumors.
Larotrectinib and entrectinib target these alterations regardless of tumor histology.
Adverse events include neurotoxicity, long half-life, and hepatotoxicity.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER MOLECULAR TARGETED THERAPY (part 3)

items.title: VEGFR Antagonists in Cancer Treatment
items.content: VEGFR antagonists like sorafenib, regorafenib, pazopanib, and sunitinib are used to treat various solid tumors including renal cell carcinoma and GISTs. These drugs inhibit VEGFR tyrosine kinase activity, which is crucial in tumor angiogenesis.
keyPoints: Sorafenib targets VEGFR and RAF serine-threonine protein kinase; regorafenib for relapsed advanced colon cancer.
Pazopanib and sunitinib are effective against renal carcinoma and GISTs, with sunitinib also targeting PDGFR and KIT.
Common side effects include hypertension, proteinuria, bleeding/clotting disorders, gastrointestinal perforation, posterior leukoencephalopathy, fatigue, diarrhea, and hand-foot syndrome.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER MOLECULAR TARGETED THERAPY (part 4)

items.title: CDK Inhibitors in Breast Cancer
items.content: CDK inhibitors such as palbociclib, abemaciclib, and ribociclib target CDK4 and CDK6 to prevent phosphorylation of the RB tumor-suppressor gene. They are used in ER-positive advanced breast cancers, often alongside mTOR inhibitors.
keyPoints: CDK4/6 inhibitors block entry into S-phase by preventing RB phosphorylation.
Used in ER-positive advanced breast cancer, frequently with mTOR inhibitors.
Potential for broader application in other RB intact tumors.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER MOLECULAR TARGETED THERAPY (part 4)

items.title: Proteasome Inhibitors in Hematologic Malignancies
items.content: Proteasome inhibitors like bortezomib, ixazomib, and carfilzomib disrupt protein degradation pathways, leading to apoptosis or autophagy in myeloma and lymphoma. They are characterized by specific side effects.
keyPoints: Boronic acid proteasome inhibitors (bortezomib, ixazomib) cause thrombocytopenia, gastrointestinal dysfunction, neuropathy.
Carfilzomib has less neuropathy but increased infusion reactions and cardiopulmonary risks.
Used in myeloma and lymphomas due to their ability to induce apoptosis or autophagy.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER MOLECULAR TARGETED THERAPY (part 4)

items.title: Tretinoin and Arsenic Trioxide in APL Treatment
items.content: Tretinoin targets the PML-RARα fusion protein, reversing transcriptional repression in acute promyelocytic leukemia (APL). It is used alongside arsenic trioxide, which affects PML-RARα via a redox mechanism. Monitoring for QT interval prolongation and electrolyte imbalances is crucial due to potential cardiotoxicity.
keyPoints: Tretinoin binds to the PML-RARα fusion protein in APL.
Leukemia differentiation syndrome can occur, responding to glucocorticoids and hydroxyurea.
Arsenic trioxide has a narrow therapeutic index with QT interval monitoring required.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER MOLECULAR TARGETED THERAPY (part 5)

items.title: Belzutifan for HIF2α Inhibition in Renal Carcinomas
items.content: Belzutifan antagonizes hypoxia-inducible factor 2 alpha (HIF2α), which is activated in von Hippel-Lindau disease-related neoplasms and sporadic clear cell renal carcinomas. It's generally safe but can cause anemia and hypoxemia.
keyPoints: Belzutifan targets HIF2α, relevant to VHL disease and renal carcinomas.
Potential adverse effects include anemia and hypoxemia.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER MOLECULAR TARGETED THERAPY (part 5)

items.title: mTOR Inhibitors in Cancer Treatment
items.content: mTOR inhibitors like temsirolimus, everolimus, and sirolimus are used for renal cancers, hormone receptor-positive breast cancer, and certain neuroendocrine tumors. They can cause stomatitis, fatigue, hyperlipidemia (10%), myelosuppression (10%), lung toxicity, and immunosuppression.
keyPoints: mTOR inhibitors include temsirolimus, everolimus, and sirolimus.
Used in renal cancers, hormone receptor-positive breast cancer, and neuroendocrine tumors.
Adverse effects: stomatitis, fatigue, hyperlipidemia (10%), myelosuppression (10%).
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER MOLECULAR TARGETED THERAPY (part 5)

items.title: PARP Inhibitors in BRCA-Associated and Platinum-Sensitive Cancers
items.content: PARP inhibitors, such as olaparib, exploit the synthetic lethality between PARP activity and homologous recombination repair pathway mutations. They are particularly effective in treating tumors with BRCA mutations by preventing DNA damage repair, leading to cell death. Additionally, these inhibitors have shown utility in non-BRCA-mutated tumors that respond to platinum-based chemotherapy.
keyPoints: PARP acts as a synthetic lethal gene with homologous recombination repair pathway mutations, including BRCA.
PARP inhibitors like olaparib are useful for treating BRCA-induced tumors and some platinum-sensitive tumors.
Sensitivity to DNA-directed cytotoxic drugs is related to the tumor's endogenous DNA repair capacity.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER MOLECULAR TARGETED THERAPY (part 6)

items.title: Targeted Therapies and Associated Risks
items.content: High-affinity targeted therapies, such as IL-3-diphtheria toxin fusion protein tagraxofusp-erzs for CD123-positive tumors and somatostatin receptor-lutetium conjugates, deliver toxic agents or radiation specifically to tumor cells. However, they can cause significant side effects like capillary leak syndrome, pulmonary dysfunction, renal damage, myelosuppression, and risk of secondary hematopoietic tumors.
keyPoints: IL-3-diphtheria toxin fusion protein targets CD123 in blastic plasmacytoid dendritic cell neoplasms but can cause capillary leak syndrome.
177-lutetium conjugates target somatostatin receptors or PSMA, with risks of renal damage and secondary hematopoietic tumors.
Ziv-aflibercept targets VEGF receptor binding domain, similar to bevacizumab in side effects.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER MOLECULAR TARGETED THERAPY (part 6)

items.title: Mechanisms of Drug Resistance in Cancer Treatment
items.content: Resistance to conventional cytotoxic agents often involves defects in drug uptake, metabolism, or export by tumor cells. The multidrug resistance (MDR) gene encoding P-glycoprotein is a key transporter that excretes drugs from tumor cells, yet no effective modulator of this process exists clinically. Drug-metabolizing enzymes like cytidine deaminase are upregulated in resistant tumors, leading to strategies such as 'high-dose cytarabine' regimens for leukemia. Increased expression or mutation of drug targets can also confer resistance, exemplified by dihydrofolate reductase gene amplification and topoisomerase II mutations.
keyPoints: MDR gene encodes P-glycoprotein, a transporter that excretes drugs from tumor cells.
No clinically useful modulator of P-glycoprotein exists yet.
'High-dose cytarabine' regimens are used in leukemia due to upregulated drug-metabolizing enzymes like cytidine deaminase.
Resistance via increased expression or mutation of drug targets, e.g., dihydrofolate reductase gene amplification and topoisomerase II mutations.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · RESISTANCE TO CANCER TREATMENTS

items.title: Apoptosis and Target Mutation in Cancer Resistance
items.content: Resistance mechanisms can involve the loss of cellular apoptotic pathways activated by drug-target engagement, influenced by tumor biology. For instance, decreased expression of alkylguanine alkyltransferase is associated with glioblastoma patients benefiting from temozolomide but not epithelial neoplasms. Ovarian cancers resistant to platinating agents show reduced BAX gene expression. Additionally, mutations in oncogenic kinase targets, such as p210^br-ab1 T315I, reflect tumor heterogeneity and subclonal evolution, impacting the efficacy of targeted therapies like sotorasib.
keyPoints: Loss of apoptotic mechanisms can lead to resistance; e.g., decreased alkylguanine alkyltransferase in glioblastoma.
Ovarian cancers resistant to platinating agents show decreased BAX expression.
Mutations in oncogenic kinase targets (e.g., p210^br-ab1 T315I) reflect tumor heterogeneity and impact targeted therapy efficacy.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · RESISTANCE TO CANCER TREATMENTS

items.title: Timing and Management of Neutropenia from Chemotherapy
items.content: Cytotoxic chemotherapeutic agents can lead to myelosuppression, with neutrophils being the most susceptible. Maximal neutropenia typically occurs 6-14 days after administration of anthracyclines, antifolates, and antimetabolites. Delayed marrow toxicity is seen with nitrosoureas, DTIC, temozolomide, and procarbazine, appearing about 6 weeks post-dosing.
keyPoints: Neutropenia peaks 6-14 days after anthracyclines, antifolates, antimetabolites
Delayed marrow toxicity with nitrosoureas, DTIC, temozolomide, procarbazine at ~6 weeks
Febrile neutropenia: fever and <1500 granulocytes/μL
CSFs like G-CSF and GM-CSF are used to boost PMN production
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · MYELOSUPPRESSION

items.title: Guidelines for Thrombocytopenia Management in Chemotherapy Patients
items.content: Thrombocytopenia is less common with solid tumors but frequent in hematologic neoplasms. Severe bleeding risks increase at platelet counts <20,000/μL in acute leukemia and <10,000/μL in solid tumors. Transfusion is generally recommended when platelet count falls to 10,000/μL or below unless there are additional risk factors for bleeding.
keyPoints: Thrombocytopenia more common in hematologic neoplasms
Severe bleeding at <20,000/μL (acute leukemia), <10,000/μL (solid tumors)
Transfusion trigger: platelet count ≤10,000/μL without comorbidities
Avoid NSAIDs and maintain clotting factors to reduce bleeding risk
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · MYELOSUPPRESSION

items.title: Identifying Chemotherapy-Induced Nausea Risk Levels and Appropriate Antiemetic Strategies
items.content: Chemotherapy-induced nausea can be classified based on the emetogenic potential of the drugs used. Highly emotogenic drugs have a risk of emesis greater than 90%, while moderately emotogenic drugs range from 30% to 90%. Low-risk agents present less than 10% risk. The choice of antiemetic regimen is determined by these risk levels, with high-risk regimens requiring combination therapy involving serotonin antagonists (5-HT3), NK1 receptor antagonists, and dexamethasone.
keyPoints: Highly emotogenic drugs (>90% risk): DTIC, cyclophosphamide >1500 mg/m², cisplatin
Moderately emotogenic drugs (30–90% risk): carboplatin, cytosine arabinoside (>1 g/m²), ifosfamide, conventional-dose cyclophosphamide, anthracyclines
Low-risk agents (<10% risk): 5-FU, taxanes, etoposide, bortezomib
High-risk regimen antiemetics: 5-HT3 blocker (e.g., dolasetron 100 mg), dexamethasone (12 mg initially, then 8 mg on days 2 and 3), NK1 antagonist (aprepitant 125 mg initially, then 80 mg)
Moderate-risk regimen antiemetics: 5-HT3 antagonist plus dexamethasone
Low-emetic-risk regimens may use dexamethasone alone or other non-5-HT3/NK1 approaches
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · NAUSEA AND VOMITING

items.title: Alternative Antiemetic Options for Chemotherapy-Induced Nausea Management
items.content: For patients receiving chemotherapy regimens with varying emetogenic risks, alternative antiemetic options are available. Antidopaminergic phenothiazines and butyrophenones can be used to target the chemoreceptor trigger zone (CTZ) in the brainstem medulla. Metoclopramide enhances gastric emptying by acting on peripheral dopamine receptors, while 5-9-tetrahydrocannabinol (Marinol) offers mild antiemetic effects for persistent nausea. Olanzapine may be beneficial for refractory cases or as part of a combination regimen in highly emetogenic chemotherapy.
keyPoints: Antidopaminergic phenothiazines: prochlorperazine, thiethylperazine
Haloperidol: 1 mg intramuscularly or orally every 8 h
Metoclopramide: high doses for highly emetogenic regimens (1–2 mg/kg), lower doses for moderately emetogenic regimens
5-9-Tetrahydrocannabinol (Marinol): 10 mg every 3–4 h as needed
Olanzapine: useful in refractory cases or with NK1 antagonist, 5-HT3 antagonist, and dexamethasone for highly emetogenic regimens
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · NAUSEA AND VOMITING

items.question: Which imaging modality is NOT mentioned as a method for detecting or confirming a tumor mass?
items.explanation: The passage mentions x-ray, MRI, and ultrasound as modalities for detecting or confirming a tumor mass, but not PET.

items.subtopic: Principles of Cancer Treatment | CANCER PRESENTATION | DETECTION OF A CANCER | ESTABLISHING A CANCER DIAGNOSIS
items.question: What is the primary reason for considering cancer in patients with persistent signs or symptoms?
options: The fraction of patients with cancer presenting these signs is high.
Missing an early-stage tumor can have serious implications.
All persistent signs are indicative of cancer.
Cancer is always the underlying cause of such symptoms.
items.explanation: Although the fraction of patients with cancer as the basis for a presenting sign or symptom may be low, missing an early-stage tumor has serious implications.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · Principles of Cancer Treatment | CANCER PRESENTATION | DETECTION OF A CANCER | ESTABLISHING A CANCER DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Principles of Cancer Treatment | CANCER PRESENTATION | DETECTION OF A CANCER | ESTABLISHING A CANCER DIAGNOSIS
items.question: Which type of biopsy is considered the procedure of second choice if an excisional biopsy cannot be performed?
items.explanation: If an excisional biopsy cannot be performed, incisional biopsy is the procedure of second choice.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · Principles of Cancer Treatment | CANCER PRESENTATION | DETECTION OF A CANCER | ESTABLISHING A CANCER DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Principles of Cancer Treatment | CANCER PRESENTATION | DETECTION OF A CANCER | ESTABLISHING A CANCER DIAGNOSIS
items.question: Which genetic abnormality mentioned in the passage may suggest valuable treatment courses for tumors without curative potential?
items.explanation: The intactness of DNA repair genes, such as BRCA1/2, may suggest valuable treatment courses in tumors without curative potential.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · Principles of Cancer Treatment | CANCER PRESENTATION | DETECTION OF A CANCER | ESTABLISHING A CANCER DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which component of the TNM staging system is related to the presence of metastatic sites?
items.explanation: The 'M' component in the TNM staging system indicates the presence of local or distant metastatic sites.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER STAGING | CANCER TREATMENT

items.question: What is the primary goal when curative cancer treatment is not possible?
items.explanation: When cure is not possible, the goal shifts to palliation: amelioration of symptoms and preservation of quality of life.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER STAGING | CANCER TREATMENT

items.question: Which cancers are not effectively staged using the TNM system?
items.explanation: The TNM staging system is not useful in diseases like leukemia, where bone marrow infiltration is never localized.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER STAGING | CANCER TREATMENT

items.question: What is the usual time of diagnosis for many solid tumors based on tumor burden?
items.explanation: The usual time of diagnosis is when the growth fraction is only 1-4% for many solid tumors.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER STAGING | CANCER TREATMENT

items.question: What is the primary goal of a curative cancer surgery?
options: To remove all regional lymph nodes regardless of their involvement.
To excise the tumor completely with an adequate margin of normal tissue.
To perform palliative care by inserting central venous catheters.
To use laparoscopic approaches for all abdominal and pelvic tumors.
items.explanation: Curative cancer surgery aims to excise the tumor completely with an adequate margin of normal tissue, known as an R0 resection.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · SURGERY

items.question: What is a sentinel node approach used for in cancer surgery?
options: To remove all regional lymph nodes.
To assess lymph node spread without the risks associated with complete nodal dissection.
items.explanation: The sentinel node approach is used to assess lymph node spread using a dye or radioisotope, avoiding the risks of resecting all regional nodes.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · SURGERY

items.question: In which scenario is neoadjuvant therapy typically employed?
options: When surgery is planned as the first treatment modality.
After diagnostic biopsy, followed by chemotherapy/radiation and then surgical removal of residual masses.
For patients with limited lung metastases from osteosarcoma.
To perform limb-sparing surgery for childhood rhabdomyosarcomas.
items.explanation: Neoadjuvant therapy involves delivering chemotherapy and/or radiation after diagnostic biopsy, followed by surgical removal of residual masses.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · SURGERY

items.question: What is the role of surgery in hormonally responsive tumors?
options: To eliminate or reduce hormone production driving certain cancers.
items.explanation: In hormonally responsive tumors, surgery like oophorectomy or orchiectomy is used to eliminate or reduce hormone production that drives cancers such as breast and prostate cancer.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · SURGERY

items.question: What is the SI unit for radiation dose?
items.explanation: The Gray (Gy) is the International System (SI) unit for radiation dose.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · RADIATION (part 1)

items.question: Which type of cells are more resistant to radiation damage?
items.explanation: Hypoxic cells are more resistant to radiation damage.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · RADIATION (part 1)

items.question: What is the primary effect of megavoltage radiation in tissue?
options: Greater damage to adjacent normal tissues
items.explanation: Megavoltage radiation (>1 MeV) has very low lateral scatter, resulting in a skin-sparing effect.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · RADIATION (part 1)

items.question: Which form of radiation therapy involves encapsulated sources implanted directly into or adjacent to tumor tissues?
items.explanation: Brachytherapy involves encapsulated sources of radiation implanted directly into or adjacent to tumor tissues.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · RADIATION (part 1)

items.question: Which organ is most sensitive to radiation therapy?
items.explanation: The male testis, female ovary, and bone marrow are the most sensitive organs to radiation.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · RADIATION (part 2) | OTHER LOCALIZED CANCER TREATMENTS

items.question: What is a serious late toxicity of radiation therapy?
items.explanation: A serious late toxicity includes the development of hematologic tumors or second solid tumors.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · RADIATION (part 2) | OTHER LOCALIZED CANCER TREATMENTS

items.question: Which treatment uses intense light to generate free radicals in cancer cells?
items.explanation: Phototherapy involves using intense light on compounds taken up by cancer cells, generating free radicals.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · RADIATION (part 2) | OTHER LOCALIZED CANCER TREATMENTS

items.question: Which historical observation led to the idea of using endogenous or exogenous substances to modulate tumor growth?
options: Paul Ehrlich's observations on dyes reacting with cell components.
Toxic effects of mustard gas derivatives on bone marrow during World War I.
Tumor shrinkage in hormone-responsive tissues after oophorectomy.
Intratumoral injection of bacterial extracts leading to tumor regression.
items.explanation: Oophorectomy led to the idea that substances could modulate tumor growth by altering its regulatory biology.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · SYSTEMIC CANCER TREATMENTS

items.question: Which type of systemic cancer treatment involves small molecules targeting DNA structure or chromosome segregation?
items.explanation: Cytotoxic chemotherapy agents are small molecules that mainly target DNA structure or chromosome segregation in mitosis.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · SYSTEMIC CANCER TREATMENTS

items.question: Which systemic cancer treatment type includes genetically modified cells and tumor-infecting viruses?
items.explanation: Cancer biologic therapies include genetically modified cells and tumor-infecting viruses that can kill tumor cells and elicit immune responses.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · SYSTEMIC CANCER TREATMENTS

items.question: What is the main reason cytotoxic chemotherapeutic agents have relatively narrow therapeutic indices?
options: They target DNA and microtubules, which are present in both normal and tumor tissues.
They only affect tumor cells without impacting normal cells.
Their targets are exclusively expressed in tumor tissues.
items.explanation: Cytotoxic chemotherapeutic agents target DNA and microtubules, which are present in both normal and tumor tissues, leading to narrow therapeutic indices.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · SYSTEMIC CANCER THERAPY OVERVIEW (part 1)

items.question: What defines the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) during a phase 1 trial?
options: The highest dose that causes irreversible toxicity.
A dose with no observed side effects.
A dose that conveys greater toxicity than would be acceptable in routine practice.
The lowest dose tested in the trial.
items.explanation: Dose-limiting toxicity (DLT) defines a dose that conveys greater toxicity than would be acceptable, allowing definition of an MTD.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · SYSTEMIC CANCER THERAPY OVERVIEW (part 1)

items.question: What is the purpose of phase 3 trials in cancer drug development?
options: To determine the starting dose for human trials.
To evaluate antitumor activity in animal models.
To seek evidence of improved overall survival or time to progression compared to a control population.
To assess the molecular analysis of target expression.
items.explanation: Phase 3 trials aim to provide evidence of improved overall survival or time to progression with the new drug compared to a control.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · SYSTEMIC CANCER THERAPY OVERVIEW (part 1)

items.question: What is considered a complete response in cancer treatment?
options: A decrease of at least 50% in tumor size.
An increase of more than 25% in the size of existing lesions.
Stabilization of tumor size without any change.
items.explanation: A complete response is defined as the disappearance of all detectable tumors.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · SYSTEMIC CANCER THERAPY OVERVIEW (part 1)

items.question: Which outcome is NOT a result of systemic cancer therapy as described in the passage?
options: Induction of cancer cell death resulting in tumor shrinkage
Increase in time until disease progression
Permanent cure for all types of cancer
Induction of cancer cell differentiation or dormancy
items.explanation: The passage does not claim that systemic therapy results in a permanent cure for all types of cancer.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · SYSTEMIC CANCER THERAPY OVERVIEW (part 2)

items.question: What is the primary difference between targeted agents and conventional chemotherapy agents?
options: Targeted agents cause macromolecular lesions indiscriminately.
Targeted agents regulate specific driver molecules of cancer.
Conventional agents are more effective than targeted agents.
Conventional agents do not interact with cell death pathways.
items.explanation: Targeted agents specifically regulate the action of driver molecules, unlike conventional chemotherapy which causes macromolecular lesions indiscriminately.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · SYSTEMIC CANCER THERAPY OVERVIEW (part 2)

items.question: In what scenario might high-dose chemotherapy regimens be used?
options: When palliative care is the primary goal.
To eliminate micrometastatic disease after surgery.
In defined clinical settings with curative potential.
For all patients regardless of tumor type.
items.explanation: High-dose chemotherapy regimens are used in defined clinical settings where they have curative potential.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · SYSTEMIC CANCER THERAPY OVERVIEW (part 2)

items.question: What is the purpose of neoadjuvant chemotherapy?
options: To provide palliative care to patients.
To enhance the effect of subsequent local treatment.
To replace surgery in cancer management.
To treat micrometastatic disease after primary therapy.
items.explanation: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy is administered before any surgery or radiation to enhance the effect of subsequent local treatment.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · SYSTEMIC CANCER THERAPY OVERVIEW (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following best describes a requirement for biologic therapies to exert their therapeutic effect?
options: Biologic therapies require no specific activity from tumor cells or host.
Biologic therapies depend on antigen expression or internalization by the tumor cell, or T-cell engagement or cytokine elaboration by the host.
Biologic therapies are effective solely based on cytotoxic mechanisms.
Biologic therapies function independently of molecular targets.
items.explanation: Biologic therapies require activity such as antigen expression or internalization by tumor cells, or T-cell engagement or cytokine elaboration by the host.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER BIOLOGIC THERAPY (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is a strategy used in antibody-mediated therapeutic approaches?
options: Antibodies are used to directly kill tumor cells without engaging host immune mechanisms.
Bispecific tumor engaging antibodies bind both to a tumor cell and a host immune cell.
Immunoregulatory antibodies target antigens on the tumor cells themselves.
All antibody strategies involve direct cytotoxic effects.
items.explanation: Bispecific tumor engaging (BiTe) antibodies directly bind to a tumor cell and to a host immune cell.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER BIOLOGIC THERAPY (part 1)

items.question: What is an unintended side effect of anti-B-cell-directed antibodies?
options: They can lead to increased opportunistic infections.
They cause immediate hypersensitivity reactions that are irreversible.
They prevent the activation of killer cells.
items.explanation: Anti-B-cell-directed antibodies can exacerbate immunosuppression, leading to increased opportunistic infections.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER BIOLOGIC THERAPY (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is a potential side effect of anti-EGFR antibodies?
options: They lead to an acneiform rash and photosensitivity.
They result in immediate cardiac arrest.
items.explanation: Anti-EGFR antibodies can cause an acneiform rash requiring treatment, as well as photosensitivity.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER BIOLOGIC THERAPY (part 1)

items.question: Which antibody is constructed to target both CD19 on cancer cells and CD3 on T cells?
items.explanation: Blinatumomab is designed to target both CD19 on cancer cells and CD3 on T cells.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER BIOLOGIC THERAPY (part 2)

items.question: What are the unique toxicities associated with blinatumomab?
options: Cytokine release syndrome and neurologic deterioration
items.explanation: Blinatumomab's unique toxicities include cytokine release syndrome and neurologic deterioration.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER BIOLOGIC THERAPY (part 2)

items.question: Which antibody is directed against the anti-CTLA4 receptor on T cells?
items.explanation: Ipilimumab targets the anti-CTLA4 receptor on T cells.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER BIOLOGIC THERAPY (part 2)

items.question: What is a potential side effect of bevacizumab?
items.explanation: Bevacizumab can cause hypertension as a side effect.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · CANCER BIOLOGIC THERAPY (part 2)

items.question: What is the primary purpose of co-administering GM-CSF in tumor vaccines?
options: To stimulate immune effector cell response
To inhibit viral replication within tumors
To prevent infection by virus types
items.explanation: GM-CSF is co-administered to enhance the capacity of antigen-presenting cells to present antigens and stimulate an immune response.
items.reference: Principles of Cancer Treatment · B. Advanced Cancers with Insufficient Data to Support Host-Derived T Lymphocyte or Immunoregulatory Antibody Treatment $ ^{d} $ (part 2)
